mnemonics: P(A,B) = P(A|B)P(B) - Remember 'Probability of A AND B is A given B times B'
keyPoints: Joint probability P(A,B) represents the probability of both events A and B occurring simultaneously
Joint distributions can be represented in tables for discrete random variables
Marginal probabilities are found by summing joint probabilities over the other variable
commonMistakes: Confusing joint probability P(A,B) with conditional probability P(A|B)
Forgetting that joint probabilities must sum to 1 across all possible outcomes
Assuming independence when it doesn't exist: P(A,B) ≠ P(A)P(B) unless A and B are independent
